preamble.summary: Psalm 70 is a compact, urgent prayer for rescue. Nearly identical to Psalm 40:14-18, it stands as an independent plea for God to hurry. The psalmist asks God to deliver him and come quickly to his help. He prays that those who seek his life will be put to shame and driven back in humiliation, while those who seek God will rejoice and say 'God is great!' The psalm closes with the psalmist identifying himself as poor and needy, calling God his help and deliverer, and begging God not to delay.
preamble.remarkable: The brevity of this psalm is itself remarkable. At only six verses (five in Hebrew), it is one of the shortest psalms in the collection and functions almost as a distress signal — concentrated, repeatable, and memorizable. The repetition of 'hurry' (chushah) frames the psalm as a plea against delay. There is no extended complaint, no detailed description of enemies, no theological argument — just the raw request for speed. The psalm's near-duplicate relationship with Psalm 40:14-18 raises questions about how psalms circulated and were adapted for different liturgical purposes. As an independent unit, it reads as a prayer that could be used in any moment of sudden crisis.
preamble.friction: The superscription identifies this as 'of David, to bring to remembrance' (lehazkir). The same notation appears on Psalm 38. The phrase may indicate a memorial offering context or simply a prayer for God to 'remember' the supplicant. The relationship to Psalm 40:14-18 is debated: did Psalm 70 extract these verses, or did Psalm 40 incorporate an originally independent prayer? Minor textual differences exist between the two versions (Psalm 70 uses Elohim where Psalm 40 uses YHWH in some instances). The brevity means that every word carries significant weight, making even small textual variants theologically relevant.
preamble.connections: The near-duplicate in Psalm 40:14-18 provides the primary literary connection. The plea for divine haste connects to the wider Psalter theme of waiting versus urgency — Psalm 62 emphasizes silent waiting, while Psalm 70 emphasizes speed. The phrase 'poor and needy' (ani ve-evyon) appears across the Psalms (9:18, 35:10, 37:14, 40:18, 72:13, 86:1, 109:22) as a self-identification by the righteous sufferer. The petition for enemies to be 'turned back' and 'put to shame' echoes Psalm 35:4 and 35:26.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm of David, to bring to remembrance.
verses.rendering: For the director of music. Of David. For remembrance.
translator_notes: The notation lehazkir ('for remembrance, to bring to remembrance') may indicate a liturgical function connected to the memorial offering (azkarah, Leviticus 2:2) or may simply designate this as a prayer requesting God's attention and memory.

verses.text_kjv: Make haste, O God, to deliver me; make haste to help me, O LORD.
verses.rendering:
God, come to rescue me!
LORD, hurry to help me!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with maximum urgency — no preamble, no setting of the scene, just a direct cry for divine intervention. The verb chushah ('hurry, hasten, come quickly') is the psalm's signature word, appearing again at the close (verse 6). Two divine names are invoked in parallel: Elohim for the rescue (lehatsileni, 'to deliver me') and YHWH for the help (le-ezrati, 'for my help'). The doubling of names intensifies the plea — the psalmist calls on God by every name he knows.
translator_notes: The verb chushah is an imperative demanding speed. This is the same verb used in Psalm 22:20, 38:23, and 71:12 — the standard emergency word for calling God to act without delay.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵבֹ֣שׁוּ וְיַחְפְּרוּ֮ מְבַקְשֵׁ֢י נַ֫פְשִׁ֥י יִסֹּ֣גוּ אָ֭חוֹר וְיִכָּלְמ֑וּ חֲ֝פֵצֵ֗י רָעָתִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them be ashamed and confounded that seek after my soul: let them be turned backward, and put to confusion, that desire my hurt.
translator_notes: Four verbs of shame and defeat accumulate: yevoshu ('let them be ashamed'), yachperu ('let them be humiliated, let them blush'), yissogu achor ('let them be turned backward, driven back'), and yikkallemu ('let them be disgraced'). The enemies are identified by their intent: mevaqshey nafshi ('seekers of my life') and chafetsey ra'ati ('those who desire my harm'). The prayer does not ask for their death but for their defeat and public embarrassment.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭שׁוּבוּ עַל־עֵ֣קֶב בׇּשְׁתָּ֑ם הָ֝אֹמְרִ֗ים הֶ֘אָ֥ח ׀ הֶאָֽח׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them be turned back for a reward of their shame that say, Aha, aha.
verses.rendering:
Let those who say "Aha! Aha!"
be turned back because of their shame.
translator_notes: The exclamation he'ach he'ach ('Aha! Aha!') is a taunt — the gloating of those who take pleasure in the psalmist's suffering. The prayer asks that their gloating be reversed: they will retreat (yashuvu, 'let them turn back') on account of their own boshtam ('shame'). The mockers will be mocked.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֘שִׂ֤ישׂוּ וְיִשְׂמְח֨וּ ׀ בְּךָ֗ כׇּל־מְבַקְשֶׁ֑יךָ וְיֹאמְר֥וּ תָ֝מִ֗יד יִגְדַּ֥ל אֱלֹהִ֗ים אֹהֲבֵ֥י יְשׁוּעָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let all those that seek thee rejoice and be glad in thee: and let such as love thy salvation say continually, Let God be magnified.
verses.expanded_rendering: The contrast between verses 3-4 and verse 5 is total: the enemies are driven back in shame, while those who seek God celebrate. The phrase kol mevaqshekha ('all who seek you') mirrors mevaqshey nafshi ('seekers of my life') from verse 3 — both groups are seeking, but what they seek determines their outcome. Those who seek the psalmist's life are shamed; those who seek God rejoice. The perpetual declaration yigdal Elohim ('God is great, let God be magnified') is the anthem of the faithful — not a one-time shout but a tamid ('always, continually') commitment.
translator_notes: The two joy verbs yasisu ('let them be joyful') and yismechu ('let them be glad') match the two shame verbs of verse 3, creating structural balance. The phrase ohavey yeshuatekha ('those who love your deliverance') identifies the faithful as people who love not just God but specifically God's saving action.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֤י ׀ עָנִ֣י וְאֶבְי֗וֹן אֱלֹהִ֥ים חֽוּשָׁ֫ה־לִּ֥י עֶזְרִ֣י וּמְפַלְטִ֣י אַ֑תָּה יְ֝הוָ֗ה אַל־תְּאַחַֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: But I am poor and needy: make haste unto me, O God: thou art my help and my deliverer; O LORD, make no tarrying.
verses.rendering:
But I — I am poor and needy.
God, hurry to me!
You are my help and my deliverer;
LORD, do not delay!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm closes as it opened — with a cry for speed — but now the psalmist has stated his identity. He is ani ve-evyon ('poor and needy'), the standard self-identification of the righteous sufferer throughout the Psalms. This is not mere social description but a theological claim: the poor and needy are the ones God has committed himself to hear (Psalm 69:34). The two titles for God — ezri ('my help') and mefalti ('my deliverer, my escape route') — compress the psalmist's entire theology into two words. The final imperative al te'achar ('do not delay, do not be late') echoes the opening chushah ('hurry') and frames the psalm as a single sustained plea for divine urgency.
translator_notes: The word mefalti ('my deliverer') comes from the root p-l-t ('to escape, to deliver') and suggests God as the one who provides a way out. The final verb te'achar ('delay, tarry, be late') treats God's intervention as time-sensitive — the psalmist's situation cannot wait.

key_terms.semantic_range: poor, afflicted, humble / needy, destitute, in want
key_terms.note: This word pair functions as a theological identity marker in the Psalms — those who are materially or socially vulnerable and who depend entirely on God for rescue. It is a position of weakness that becomes a position of access to God.
